findings.title: Pin and verify the installer artifact
findings.whyItBreaks: The added line downloads a mutable network response and pipes it directly to `sh`, so upstream content changes or endpoint compromise can execute arbitrary commands during image builds.

findings.title: Pin and verify the downloaded installer
findings.whyItBreaks: The newly added build step downloads and executes content from a mutable remote URL during every image build. If that response changes or the endpoint is compromised, arbitrary commands can run in the build environment and be embedded in the image.

findings.title: Pin and verify the downloaded installer
findings.whyItBreaks: Every image build fetches and executes whatever content example.com serves at build time. A compromised, changed, or intercepted response gains arbitrary code execution in the build and can embed it in the shipped image; the URL is neither version-pinned nor integrity-verified.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key status in the exported helper
findings.whyItBreaks: The newly exported function explicitly ignores Load's ErrMissing result. An absent key and a present key with an empty value both return "", so consumers cannot distinguish lookup failure from stored data.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors in the public helper
findings.whyItBreaks: The new exported helper discards Load's ErrMissing and returns its string zero value. An absent key therefore produces "", identical to a present key mapped to an empty string.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors in LoadOrDefault
findings.whyItBreaks: The new exported function discards Load's ErrMissing result and returns "". Since the map can store "", consumers cannot distinguish a missing key from a present empty value.

findings.title: Wait for result sends before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: Each job is now sent from an untracked goroutine, so Run returns immediately instead of returning only after all results have been delivered. A caller that closes results after Run returns can race these sends and panic with "send on closed channel"; callers consuming after Run also observe incomplete results.

findings.title: Wait for worker sends before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: Run previously returned only after every value had been sent. It now returns immediately after launching goroutines, so a caller that closes results after Run returns can race with those sends and panic with "send on closed channel". Callers also can no longer use Run returning as completion.

findings.title: Emit the supplied log tag
findings.whyItBreaks: Log("message", "tag") produces exactly the same output as Log("message"); callers cannot attach the tag exposed by the public API.

findings.title: Require admin status and tenant match before deleting records
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `||` predicate returns true when either condition holds, allowing non-admin users in the record's tenant and administrators from other tenants. The previous predicate required both conditions.

findings.title: Require both admin role and matching tenant for deletion
findings.whyItBreaks: Using || authorizes any non-admin session whose tenant matches the record, and also authorizes an admin whose tenant does not match. Both cases bypass the prior tenant-scoped administrator requirement for deletion.

findings.title: Require admin status before allowing record deletion
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `||` expression returns true for `isAdmin=false` whenever the session and record tenant IDs match, allowing same-tenant non-admin users through this exported delete authorization predicate.

findings.title: Preserve parse failures instead of overwriting saved settings
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch turns JSON and field-validation failures into defaults. `updateFontSize` then serializes and saves those defaults with the requested size, replacing an invalid persisted value rather than preserving the previous error behavior.

findings.title: Preserve parse failures instead of overwriting settings
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch converts invalid JSON or invalid settings fields into defaults. updateFontSize then saves those defaults at src/settings.ts:22, overwriting malformed settings instead of preserving the previous parse/validation failure.

findings.title: Preserve parse failures instead of overwriting settings
findings.whyItBreaks: Malformed or invalid persisted settings previously threw before updateFontSize could call save. They now become DEFAULTS; updateFontSize then changes fontSize and saves the replacement, silently overwriting the invalid/corrupt settings with theme "light".

findings.title: Use the requested commit for report artifacts
findings.whyItBreaks: `builtFrom` is resolved from the requested `branch`, but the changed return line fetches `artifacts` from `build.ref("verified")`. The resulting report can identify one commit while returning artifacts from another.

findings.title: Load artifacts from the report's built commit
findings.whyItBreaks: When branch differs from "verified", builtFrom identifies the requested branch commit while artifacts are fetched from the verified commit, yielding a self-contradictory BuildReport.

findings.title: Load artifacts from the reported commit
findings.whyItBreaks: `builtFrom` is `build.ref(branch)`, but the changed return statement loads `artifacts` using `build.ref("verified")`. If those refs differ, the returned report associates verified-ref artifacts with the requested branch commit.

findings.title: Apply the limit before returning entries
findings.whyItBreaks: For every positive limit, this returns every reversed entry instead of at most `limit` as promised. `renderFeed` passes 20 at src/feed.ts:9 and iterates every returned entry, extending its initially 20-slot output when more entries exist.

findings.title: Restore the requested entry limit
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit, this returns the complete reversed input instead of at most limit entries. renderFeed passes 20 at src/feed.ts:9; its loop then writes every returned entry, expanding its initially 20-slot output array and exposing more than 20 entries.

findings.title: Restore enforcement of the requested entry limit
findings.whyItBreaks: For a positive limit smaller than all.length, this returns every reversed entry instead of at most limit. renderFeed passes 20 at src/feed.ts:9 and writes every returned entry, extending its 20-slot array when there are more than 20 inputs.

findings.title: Count only emitted manifest rows
findings.whyItBreaks: The new stale-entry filter omits stale entries from the manifest rows, while the header still declares `entries.length`. A manifest containing stale entries therefore reports more data rows than it emits.

findings.title: Count only emitted manifest entries
findings.whyItBreaks: When any input entry has stale=true, it is omitted from rows but the header still uses entries.length. The manifest therefore declares more entries than it contains.

findings.title: Keep the header count aligned with emitted rows
findings.whyItBreaks: The new filter at line 20 omits stale entries from emitted rows, while the return still supplies the unfiltered `entries.length` to `# entry-count`. Consumers receive a manifest whose declared count can exceed its data rows.

findings.title: Restore FormatLegacyID for downstream compatibility
findings.whyItBreaks: Deleting exported ids.FormatLegacyID makes downstream consumers that call it fail to compile with an undefined-symbol error. Retaining a deprecated wrapper preserves the package's public API while consumers migrate.

findings.title: Preserve the shippingQuote export
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed hunk removes the exported shippingQuote function from src/orders.ts. Existing external consumers importing shippingQuote from this module will fail to compile or load after upgrade.

findings.title: Preserve the shippingQuote public export
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed hunk removes the exported shippingQuote function from src/orders.ts. Existing external consumers importing { shippingQuote } from this module will no longer compile or resolve that named export.

findings.title: Preserve the shippingQuote export
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed hunk removes the exported shippingQuote function from this module. Existing consumers importing shippingQuote from src/orders will fail to compile or resolve that export after upgrade.

findings.title: Restore execute's string result
findings.whyItBreaks: Every call to the exported execute(input) function now throws "always fails"; the prior implementation returned the input uppercased, so all consumers lose the function's only successful behavior.

findings.title: Restore execute's string result
findings.whyItBreaks: Every call to execute now throws "always fails", so the previously working input-to-uppercase transformation cannot complete for any valid string.

findings.title: Restore execute's promised string result
findings.whyItBreaks: execute is exported with a return type of string, but every invocation now throws Error("always fails") and no caller can receive a string result.

findings.title: Preserve the read-only path for eligible viewers
findings.whyItBreaks: For an active viewer with fewer than five attempts, isEligible now returns false, so handle returns "rejected" at line 24. Before this change, that same request reached line 25 and returned "read-only"; the new predicate over-blocks that distinct supported action.

findings.title: Keep eligible viewers on the read-only path
findings.whyItBreaks: The new viewer-role guard makes active viewers with fewer than five attempts fail isEligible and return "rejected" from handle, instead of reaching its existing "read-only" branch.

findings.title: Preserve the viewer read-only handling
findings.whyItBreaks: An active viewer with fewer than five attempts previously passes isEligible and handle returns "read-only". The new predicate rejects that same request at handle's gate, making its viewer-specific read-only branch unreachable.

findings.title: Do not require payment to save a draft
findings.whyItBreaks: The added payment condition makes saveDraft({ state: "draft", payment: "missing" }) return "blocked". Payment can legitimately be absent while persisting a draft; only submitOrder is governed by payment readiness.

findings.title: Keep draft saves independent of payment status
findings.whyItBreaks: For an unpaid draft, the new predicate returns false, so saveDraft returns "blocked" instead of "saved". Payment legitimately governs submission, but not persisting a draft.

findings.title: Keep payment validation out of draft saves
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed predicate returns false for draft orders with payment="missing". src/actions.ts:9 calls this predicate from saveDraft, so those orders now return "blocked" instead of "saved".

findings.title: Read files from the requested channel commit
findings.whyItBreaks: For any channel whose head differs from `stable`, the returned Snapshot has `commit` set to that channel's head but `files` from stable. Consumers cannot reliably retrieve the listed files from the advertised commit.

findings.title: Load files from the selected channel commit
findings.whyItBreaks: For publishSnapshot(repo, "main"), commit remains repo.headCommit("main") while files are loaded from repo.headCommit("stable"). When those refs differ, the returned Snapshot describes one commit but contains another commit's files.

findings.title: Read files from the snapshot commit
findings.whyItBreaks: `head` is resolved from the requested `channel` and returned as `commit`, while the changed line reads `files` from a separately resolved `stable` head. A non-stable snapshot therefore reports one commit with another revision's file list.

findings.title: Honor the new limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed public signature introduces limit, but the function still unconditionally iterates range(max_count) where max_count is 10. Thus build("item", 2) returns 10 entries instead of limiting output.

findings.title: Honor the requested limit
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `limit` parameter promises that callers can control the number of generated values, but the body still unconditionally sets `max_count = 10` at line 2. For example, `build("x", 3)` returns ten values rather than three.

findings.title: Honor the limit argument
findings.whyItBreaks: Callers can now request a bounded result (for example, build("x", 2)), but the function still returns 10 elements because the changed signature's limit parameter is never used and the loop iterates the hard-coded max_count.

findings.title: Enforce positivity in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The newly named parse_positive_int still returns int(value), which accepts zero and negative integers. Its in-file consumer charge() therefore emits zero or negative cents for those inputs, contradicting the new contract.

findings.title: Reject non-positive values in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The renamed function newly promises a positive integer, but `int(value)` returns zero and negative values unchanged. Its in-file consumer, `charge` (`src/validators.py:6`), therefore produces zero or negative cents.

findings.title: Reject zero and negative values in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The renamed function now promises a positive integer, but the changed body still accepts zero and negative values. charge('-1') reaches src/validators.py:6 and returns -100 cents.

findings.title: Preserve the caller's DataFrame
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously apply_values copied df and returned the transformed copy. It now writes through to df, so callers that retain the input for later use observe unexpected value changes after calling this function.

findings.title: Preserve the input DataFrame
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed assignments now write directly to df and return that same object, whereas the base implementation assigned into df.copy(). Any caller retaining df will observe its values changed after calling this function.

findings.title: Preserve the caller DataFrame when applying mappings
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously apply_values returned a modified copy and left df unchanged. It now writes directly into df, so callers that retain or reuse their input will observe values changed before they opt into the returned result.

findings.title: Reject non-positive integers in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The new public name promises a positive integer, but `"-2"` parses successfully and returns -2. `charge` consumes that value at src/parse.rs:6 and returns -200.

findings.title: Enforce the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int("-5")` returns -5 despite its newly introduced positive-integer name; `charge` then emits a negative charge.

findings.title: Validate positivity before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int("-1")` returns `-1`, contradicting its public positive-value contract; `charge` at `src/parse.rs:6` consequently returns `-100`.

findings.title: Borrow items when emitting debug output
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` takes ownership of the `Vec<String>` and discards its returned value. The subsequent `items.len()` uses the moved value, causing Rust error E0382 and preventing compilation.

findings.title: Avoid moving items before calculating its length
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` evaluates and returns the owned Vec; the trailing semicolon drops that returned value. The subsequent `items.len()` therefore uses a moved value, causing Rust compilation to fail.

findings.title: Borrow items for debug output
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` evaluates and returns `items` by value; because that returned value is discarded, `items` is moved and line 3 subsequently borrows a moved value, causing Rust error E0382.

score.error: critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)

findings.title: Compute paths from the advertised revision
findings.whyItBreaks: When `pinnedTag` resolves differently from `latest`, `fromRevision` remains the pinned revision while `changedPaths` is computed from `latest`. The public `Changeset` contract says consumers replay paths on `fromRevision`, so callers can receive paths for the wrong base.

findings.title: Compute paths from the revision advertised by the changeset
findings.whyItBreaks: `fromRevision` is resolved from `pinnedTag`, but `changedPaths` is calculated from `latest`. When those differ, the returned paths are paired with the wrong base revision despite the interface contract promising they were computed against `fromRevision`.

findings.title: Compute changed paths from the advertised revision
findings.whyItBreaks: `fromRevision` remains the resolved pinned tag, while the changed line derives `changedPaths` from `latest`. If they differ, consumers replay paths computed against a different revision than the one the exported contract identifies.

findings.title: Update queries to use the renamed email column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, `users.email` no longer exists, but `src/query.sql:1` still executes `SELECT email FROM users`, which fails with a missing-column error.

findings.title: Update queries that still select the renamed email column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, users.email no longer exists, but src/query.sql still executes `SELECT email FROM users`; that query will fail with a missing-column error.

findings.title: Update queries for the renamed email column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, `users.email` no longer exists, but `src/query.sql:1` still executes `SELECT email FROM users;`. Any execution after upgrade fails with a missing-column error.

findings.title: Restore environment-based billing credential loading
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed line embeds a `bk_live_` credential in source control, exposing it to repository readers and history. It also makes `makeClient` ignore `BILLING_API_KEY`, so deployments cannot select their configured billing account.

findings.title: Restore BILLING_API_KEY configuration and validation
findings.whyItBreaks: `makeClient()` now always returns the hard-coded key, so deployments cannot use their configured `BILLING_API_KEY`; an absent key no longer produces the prior explicit configuration error.

findings.title: Remove the hard-coded live billing API key
findings.whyItBreaks: The `bk_live_` credential is committed in plaintext, exposing it to repository readers, clones, forks, and diff artifacts. The client also ignores `BILLING_API_KEY`, preventing deployment-time credential rotation.

findings.title: Allow admins to purge archived projects
findings.whyItBreaks: For user.isAdmin=true and project.archived=true, this returns "forbidden" before db.delete runs. The prior behavior purged exactly this authorized case, so all administrator purge requests are blocked.

findings.title: Require an admin before deleting an archived project
findings.whyItBreaks: `user.isAdmin === true` now immediately returns `forbidden`, while a non-admin with an archived project reaches `db.delete` at line 14. This reverses the prior authorization rule and permits unauthorized destructive deletion.

findings.title: Restore the admin requirement for project purging
findings.whyItBreaks: `user.isAdmin === true` now immediately returns "forbidden", while a non-admin with an archived project reaches `db.delete(project.id)`. This reverses the prior authorization behavior and permits unauthorized destructive deletion.

findings.title: Restore the missing-settings fallback
findings.whyItBreaks: load_settings returns None for a nonexistent path, and the changed return expression immediately subscripts it. startup therefore raises TypeError instead of returning the prior default worker count of 1.

findings.title: Preserve defaults when the settings file is missing
findings.whyItBreaks: load_settings returns None when path does not exist (app/config.py:6-7). Removing the None fallback makes startup evaluate settings["workers"] on None, raising TypeError instead of starting with the previous default of one worker.

findings.title: Preserve the missing-config fallback
findings.whyItBreaks: When the path does not exist, load_settings returns None. Removing the fallback causes startup to subscript None at the changed return path, raising TypeError instead of using the previous default of one worker.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported Charge API logs a Gateway.Submit failure at the changed line and then reaches `return nil`, so consumers cannot distinguish a failed payment from a completed charge.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When Gateway.Submit returns an error, Charge logs it and returns nil. Callers therefore cannot distinguish a failed charge from a completed one and may mark an unpaid order as paid.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported Charge API logs a Gateway.Submit failure at the changed line and then returns nil, so callers cannot distinguish a failed payment from a successful charge.

findings.title: Include tenantId in the cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After tenant A reads a setting named `name`, `settingsFor` stores it under `settings:name`. A subsequent request from tenant B for the same name hits that entry before calling `store.fetch`, returning tenant A's value to tenant B.

findings.title: Preserve the two-argument invalidate API
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function previously accepted `(tenantId, name)`. Existing TypeScript consumers fail to compile after upgrading; JavaScript consumers that retain two arguments silently use tenantId as the name and invalidate the wrong entry.

findings.title: Include the tenant in the settings cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: settingsFor now looks up values solely by name before calling store.fetch. After tenant A reads "theme", tenant B reading the same name receives tenant A's cached value instead of its own setting.

findings.title: Preserve tenant-scoped invalidation compatibility
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported API previously accepted (tenantId, name). Existing JavaScript callers passing those arguments now delete settings:<tenantId>, leaving the cached setting intact; TypeScript callers also fail to compile because the second argument is no longer accepted.

findings.title: Restore tenant scoping in the settings cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: `settingsFor` still fetches values using `tenantId`, but its changed cache key is only `settings:<name>`. Once tenant A caches a name, tenant B receives that cached value for the same name without querying its store, exposing tenant-specific settings and applying the wrong configuration.

findings.title: Preserve the exported invalidate call contract
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported function changed from `invalidate(tenantId, name)` to `invalidate(name)`, breaking existing TypeScript consumers that call the prior two-argument API. JavaScript consumers retaining that call shape pass the tenant ID as the new name argument, so they invalidate the wrong cache entry.

findings.title: Serialize reservations before awaiting persistence
findings.whyItBreaks: Two concurrent reserve("sku") calls can both read remaining=1, both await save(sku, 0), then both return true. Before this change the first synchronous call updated stock before the second could read it, so the second call returned false.

findings.title: Keep the existing no-argument construction path
findings.whyItBreaks: Inventory was previously constructible with new Inventory(). This required constructor is a source-breaking change for existing TypeScript consumers; JavaScript consumers can still construct it but reserve then throws when accessing undefined.save.

findings.title: Version the synchronous reserve API change
findings.whyItBreaks: Changing reserve from boolean to Promise<boolean> breaks existing callers. In JavaScript, `if (inventory.reserve(sku))` now always takes the truthy Promise branch even when stock is empty; TypeScript callers no longer type-check in boolean contexts.

findings.title: Serialize concurrent reservations per SKU
findings.whyItBreaks: With stock=1, two callers can both read remaining=1 before either await completes, both save 0, both set local stock to 0, and both resolve true. The second successful reservation has no inventory backing it.

findings.title: Preserve the no-argument synchronous Inventory API
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported constructor now requires Store, so existing `new Inventory()` consumers no longer typecheck. `reserve` also now returns a Promise, so JavaScript conditionals receive a truthy Promise rather than the reservation result.

findings.title: Make reservation atomic across the await
findings.whyItBreaks: Two concurrent reserve calls for stock=1 both read remaining=1 before either reaches the await, both save 0, and both return true. This reserves two units while persisting only one decrement.

findings.title: Update retry to consume millisecond backoff values
findings.whyItBreaks: `backoff(0)` now returns 1,000 milliseconds, but `retry` still passes `backoff(a) * 1000` to its millisecond `sleep` helper. The first retry therefore waits 1,000,000 ms (~16.7 minutes) instead of 1 second; capped retries wait 30,000,000 ms (~8.3 hours).

findings.title: Keep retry's backoff in milliseconds
findings.whyItBreaks: backoff() now returns milliseconds (1000 for attempt 0), but retry() still calls sleep(backoff(a) * 1000). The first failed attempt now waits 1,000,000 ms (16m 40s) instead of 1 second; later attempts wait up to 30 minutes before retrying or surfacing the final error.

findings.title: Keep retry delay in milliseconds
findings.whyItBreaks: backoff(0) now returns 1,000 milliseconds, but retry still calls sleep(backoff(a) * 1000). After the first failed attempt it waits 1,000,000 ms (about 16.7 minutes) instead of the previous 1,000 ms; capped retries wait 30,000,000 ms (about 8.3 hours).

findings.title: Propagate the deferred Close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The new deferred f.Close() discards its return value. Previously, Save returned a Close failure after a successful Flush; it now returns nil, weakening the exported function's error contract.

findings.title: Propagate the deferred Close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `defer f.Close()` ignores its error while the function now returns only `w.Flush()`. Before this diff, Save returned `f.Close()` after a successful flush, so callers could observe a final close/write failure.

findings.title: Propagate the file close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The new deferred `f.Close()` ignores its error, while the previous implementation returned `f.Close()` after a successful flush. Save can now report success despite a close failure.

findings.title: Include the page end row in page_window
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed helper computes a half-open end bound, but range(start, end - 1) excludes the final valid position. One-row pages produce no rows and larger pages omit their last row; render_page and summarize expose the incomplete output.

findings.title: Include the page end index when collecting rows
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed bounds helper returns a half-open end, but `range(start, end - 1)` excludes the last valid position. One-row pages become empty and larger pages lose their final row; `render_page` at lib/pagination.py:40 consequently emits incomplete output.

findings.title: Include the page end in the window iteration
findings.whyItBreaks: _window_bounds returns half-open bounds, but `range(start, end - 1)` excludes `end - 1`. Consequently, one-row pages render empty and each non-empty page loses its final row through render_page (lib/pagination.py:39) and summarize (lib/pagination.py:51).

findings.title: Return entries only before their TTL expires
findings.whyItBreaks: A newly stored entry has age near zero, so it fails `age > ttlMs`, is deleted, and returns null. Once expired, the same condition becomes true and stale data is returned indefinitely.

findings.title: Avoid skipping adjacent completed jobs while pruning
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing an element during `forEach` shifts the next job into the current index, but `forEach` then advances to the following index. For `[done, done, pending]`, the second completed job remains in the returned queue.

findings.title: Restore the TTL comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: A freshly stored entry has age 0, so it fails `0 > ttlMs`, is deleted, and returns null. Conversely, expired entries satisfy the condition and are returned indefinitely.

findings.title: Do not skip consecutive completed jobs
findings.whyItBreaks: Splicing during `forEach` shifts the next job into the current index, but `forEach` advances to the following index. For `[done, done, pending]`, the second completed job remains in the returned queue.

findings.title: Preserve the caller-owned jobs array
findings.whyItBreaks: The previous implementation returned a filtered copy. This new `splice` mutates the array supplied by callers, so callers retaining `jobs` observe completed jobs removed even if they ignore the return value.

findings.title: Return entries only while their TTL is valid
findings.whyItBreaks: A newly stored entry has age 0, so it fails `0 > ttlMs`, is deleted, and `get` returns null. Once expired, the condition becomes true and the stale value is returned.

findings.title: Remove every completed job during pruning
findings.whyItBreaks: Splicing while `forEach` advances its index skips the item shifted into the removed item's position. For `[doneA, doneB]`, removing `doneA` at index 0 causes `doneB` to move to index 0, but the next iteration checks index 1, leaving `doneB` in the returned queue.

findings.title: Restore numeric descending score comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed default sort compares numeric scores as strings before reversing them. With scores [9, 20, 100], rankScores returns [9, 20, 100], so topPlayers uses 9 as the n=1 cutoff and can return the first input player instead of the 100-score player.

findings.title: Restore numeric descending score sorting
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() without a comparator orders scores as strings. For players ordered with scores [9, 100, 8] and n=1, rankScores returns [9, 8, 100], making the cutoff 9; topPlayers then returns the first qualifying input player (score 9) instead of score 100.

findings.title: Sort scores with a numeric comparator
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() compares numbers as strings. For players ordered as scores [9, 10, 8] with n=1, rankScores returns [9, 8, 10], yielding a cutoff of 9; topPlayers then retains both 9 and 10 and slice(0, 1) returns the 9-point player instead of the numeric leader.

findings.title: Restore constant-time webhook signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed equality comparison can short-circuit on a mismatch. Since handle() exposes the verification result as a 401 or 200 response at app/webhook.py:14, it creates a timing oracle for an attacker to progressively guess an HMAC signature.

findings.title: Restore constant-time HMAC signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: Using `==` can return sooner for signatures that differ earlier, exposing prefix-dependent timing. An attacker able to measure webhook response timing can use this signal to iteratively forge a valid signature and reach `processor(payload)` through `handle`.

findings.title: Restore constant-time signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed equality comparison may return after the first differing character, making comparison time depend on how much of an attacker-controlled signature matches. `handle()` uses this result to authorize `processor(payload)`, so restore constant-time comparison for the webhook HMAC.

findings.title: Keep balance updates in the transfer transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction ends before `_apply_balances` runs, committing the ledger INSERT independently. If either subsequent account UPDATE fails or execution stops between statements, the ledger retains a transfer without its corresponding debit and/or credit. The base version kept all three writes in one transaction.

findings.title: Keep balance updates in the transfer transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction commits after `_record_ledger` exits at line 23. If either subsequent account update fails, the ledger records a completed transfer while balances are unchanged or only the source account was debited.

findings.title: Keep balance updates inside the transfer transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction block now ends immediately after recording the ledger entry, while both account UPDATEs execute afterward. A failure during either UPDATE can leave the ledger entry committed without matching balances, or leave only the debit applied.

findings.title: Preserve UTC billing-day calculation
findings.whyItBreaks: This replaces the previous UTC getters with local-time getters. The same instant, such as `2026-01-01T00:30:00Z`, can therefore be assigned to different billing dates depending on the server timezone.

findings.title: Keep billing-day calculation in UTC
findings.whyItBreaks: The function previously produced a canonical UTC date. getFullYear/getMonth/getDate instead use the executing machine's local timezone, so the same instant can be assigned to different billing days by workers in different zones (for example, 2026-07-18T00:30:00Z is 2026-07-17 in America/Los_Angeles). This also changes sameBillingDay results near midnight.

findings.title: Preserve UTC when deriving the billing day
findings.whyItBreaks: The prior implementation deliberately used UTC accessors. The new local-time accessors return a different billing date for the same instant on hosts outside UTC; for example, an instant shortly after 00:00 UTC is classified as the preceding day in a negative-offset timezone. This also makes sameBillingDay vary by the server's configured timezone.

findings.title: Restore tenant isolation in the profile cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After tenant A reads account X, a read for tenant B and account X returns tenant A's cached Profile without calling fetchProfile. Profile includes its tenant, so this returns data for the wrong tenant.

findings.title: Keep tenant in the profile cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After readProfile("tenant-a", "acct", ...) caches tenant-a's Profile, readProfile("tenant-b", "acct", ...) uses the same "acct" key and returns it at src/profile.ts:16 without calling fetchProfile. This crosses tenant boundaries and exposes the wrong profile data.

findings.title: Include tenant in memoization key
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `key = account` line makes requests for the same account under different tenants share the `memo` entry. The subsequent `if (cached) return cached` returns the first tenant's Profile without calling `fetchProfile` for the requested tenant.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors from load
findings.whyItBreaks: A missing key previously threw `Error("missing: ...")`; it now returns an empty string, which is also a valid Map value. `loadAll` at src/store.ts:12 therefore returns a seemingly successful array containing an indistinguishable empty value for missing keys.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key failures
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed catch block converts the explicit missing-key error into `""`. Consumers of exported `load` can no longer distinguish an absent key from a legitimately stored empty string.

findings.title: Preserve the missing-key error from load
findings.whyItBreaks: A missing key previously threw at src/store.ts:4; it now returns "", which is also a valid stored value. The exported loadAll caller maps that fallback directly into its returned array at src/store.ts:12, so consumers cannot distinguish a missing key from a key explicitly stored as an empty string.

findings.title: Preserve the existing Config fields during the API change
findings.whyItBreaks: Config is exported. Replacing required timeoutMs with required timeout and retries makes existing consumers passing the prior shape ({ maxAttempts, timeoutMs }) fail type-checking on upgrade.

findings.title: Propagate failure after retryBackground exhausts retries
findings.whyItBreaks: When fn fails on every attempt, retryBackground returns void exactly as it does after success. This exported API therefore hides terminal operation failure from callers.

findings.title: Keep the existing retry configuration shape
findings.whyItBreaks: Config is exported and still accepted by retry(), but existing callers using { maxAttempts, timeoutMs } no longer type-check. JavaScript callers retain an undefined config.timeout, making deadline NaN and disabling the timeout check.

findings.title: Run retryBackground outside the caller path
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported retryBackground name promises background execution, but it invokes fn() synchronously before returning. A slow or blocking callback therefore blocks the caller rather than being retried in the background.

findings.title: Surface exhausted retry failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When every attempt throws, retryBackground catches each exception and returns void after exhausting retries. Its caller cannot distinguish that failure from a successful first attempt and may continue as though the background operation succeeded.

findings.title: Preserve compatibility for the exported Config fields
findings.whyItBreaks: Existing callers supply timeoutMs and no retries. In JavaScript, config.timeout is then undefined, so deadline becomes NaN and Date.now() > deadline is always false; a configured zero/expired timeout no longer prevents an attempt. TypeScript callers also no longer satisfy the exported Config type.

findings.title: Expose exhaustion failures from retryBackground
findings.whyItBreaks: If every fn invocation throws, the empty catch discards each error and the exported void function returns normally after the loop. A caller cannot distinguish successful background work from exhausted failure.

findings.title: Handle numeric Field values before reading length
findings.whyItBreaks: `value` now permits `number`, but the changed expression reads `value.length`; numbers have no `length` property. This is a TypeScript error and numeric values cannot be handled correctly.

findings.title: Handle numeric values before reading length
findings.whyItBreaks: FieldProps now accepts number, but the changed expression `value.length` is undefined for numeric values. Passing value={42} renders `len=undefined`; TypeScript also rejects property access on `string | number`.

findings.title: Handle numeric Field values before reading length
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed public prop type permits `number`, while the changed body reads `value.length`. TypeScript rejects property access on `string | number`; if emitted without type checking, `<Field value={12} />` produces `len=undefined`.

findings.title: Restore sufficient buffering for generated patches
findings.whyItBreaks: `git(["diff", mergeBase, headSha], cwd)` materializes the full PR patch at `src/adapters/github.ts:140`. Removing the explicit 64 MiB `maxBuffer` restores `spawnSync`'s 1 MiB default, causing large diffs to fail before `runGithub` enters its error handler.

findings.title: Retain the enlarged buffer for Git output
findings.whyItBreaks: `git(["diff", mergeBase, headSha])` at line 140 collects the full PR patch. Without `maxBuffer`, `spawnSync` uses its 1 MiB default, so larger patches fail before review execution and the catch path posts a failed check. The removed 64 MiB limit supported these inputs.

findings.title: Retain the 64 MiB buffer for Git diff output
findings.whyItBreaks: runGithub retrieves the complete PR patch with `git diff` at src/adapters/github.ts:143. Without `maxBuffer`, `spawnSync` uses Node’s 1 MiB default; a larger textual diff causes the Git command to fail, which aborts the review and reports it as failed.

findings.title: Keep the Codex subprocess read-only
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing `-s read-only` allows the Codex process launched in `opts.repoPath` to use its configured/default sandbox policy, which can permit workspace writes. A PR-review prompt and diff are untrusted input; the reviewer can alter the checked-out repository rather than only inspect it, contaminating review results and potentially changing files later automation consumes.

findings.title: Restore the read-only sandbox for Codex reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed args no longer pass `-s read-only`, while `spawnSync` runs Codex with `opts.repoPath` as its cwd. Codex is therefore no longer explicitly constrained from writing to the repository it is reviewing.

findings.title: Restore the explicit read-only sandbox
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed args no longer pass `-s read-only`, so review execution inherits the installed Codex sandbox/default configuration. Since Codex is launched with `cwd: opts.repoPath`, a write-capable configuration can modify the repository being reviewed; the prior invocation explicitly prevented that.

findings.title: Build the review diff from the PR head and merge base
findings.whyItBreaks: PR_HEAD_SHA is still used to attach the review/check at src/adapters/github.ts:178-179, but the patch and changed-file set now use HEAD at lines 139-140. When Actions checks out a merge ref, the bundle can include merge/base-only changes or omit the configured PR head's diff, causing Needlefish to review different code from the commit it reports on.

findings.title: Diff the intended PR head from its merge base
findings.whyItBreaks: When PR_BASE_SHA is a later base-branch tip or HEAD is a merge checkout, `git diff baseSha HEAD` includes changes outside the PR head while review results are posted against `headSha`. The previous merge-base-to-headSha range isolated the PR's changes.

findings.title: Diff the PR from its merge-base
findings.whyItBreaks: When PR_BASE_SHA is the current base-branch tip and a PR branch is behind it, `git diff baseSha HEAD` includes base-only changes. Those files and hunks enter the review bundle and may receive review comments despite not belonging to the PR.

findings.title: Preserve literal prompt payloads during replacement
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed calls pass arbitrary JSON and patch text as String.prototype.replace replacement strings. Replacement tokens such as `$&`, `$`` , `$'`, and `$$` are interpreted, so submitted diff text or serialized findings can be altered before reaching the review or critic prompt.

findings.title: Keep prompt payloads in functional replacements
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed string-replacement arguments interpret replacement patterns such as $&, $`, $', and $$ rather than inserting them literally. Since bundle.patch and serialized bundle/candidate data can contain these tokens, the prompts passed to runCodex can be altered before review or critique.

findings.title: Preserve dollar sequences when interpolating prompt data
findings.whyItBreaks: String.replace expands $&, $`, $', and $$ in string replacement arguments. Because bundle.patch and serialized bundle/candidate data are injected this way, a patch containing these sequences is not delivered verbatim to the review or critic prompt, allowing PR content to corrupt or hide review context.

findings.title: Preserve the reviewed commit in the fallback
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed fallback request omits `commit_id`. The review bundle is built for `headSha` before `await review(bundle)`, but the fallback posts only afterward. If the PR advances during review execution and the inline-comment request fails, GitHub can associate the fallback review with the newer, unreviewed PR head.

findings.title: Keep the fallback review pinned to the reviewed SHA
findings.whyItBreaks: If the initial review POST fails and the PR advances before the fallback runs, omitting `commit_id` lets GitHub attach the fallback review to the then-current tip. The review result and check run remain based on `headSha`, so the fallback can label newer, unreviewed code.

findings.title: Preserve the reviewed SHA in the fallback POST
findings.whyItBreaks: When the JSON submission fails, this fallback omits commit_id. GitHub defaults the review to the most recent PR commit, while the result was computed against headSha captured before analysis. A push between those calls can attach a stale verdict to newer, unreviewed code.

findings.title: Reject malformed findings instead of fabricating defaults
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid or missing required fields now become a Finding with values such as severity="P1", file="", and lineStart=0 rather than raising the malformed-output error. The exported normalizer therefore forces callers to treat failed validation as a real finding and can emit unusable locations.

findings.title: Preserve the strict=false review-normalization contract
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the optional strict parameter breaks existing TypeScript callers that pass false. JavaScript callers that used false to discard malformed findings now retain fabricated findings instead, changing the previously supported permissive behavior.

findings.title: Reject malformed finding fields instead of fabricating defaults
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid severity/category and missing required metadata previously raised a malformed-finding error. They now become plausible-looking values such as severity="P1", category="bug", file="", and lineStart=0, so consumers cannot distinguish invalid model output from a real finding.

findings.title: Preserve the exported strict-mode parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the public second parameter breaks TypeScript callers using normalizeReview(raw, false). JavaScript callers still pass the argument but it is ignored; their prior lenient behavior of dropping malformed findings is replaced by retaining fabricated defaults.

findings.title: Reject malformed findings before returning a Finding
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid severities and missing required fields now produce defaulted Finding values (for example P1, bug, empty file, and line 0) instead of an error. Consumers of this exported normalization API can no longer distinguish malformed review output from a real finding.

findings.title: Restore explicit repository selection for manual dispatches
findings.whyItBreaks: The workflow_dispatch path has no pull_request event SHA, so it runs these gh commands before any checkout. After removing both REPO and `-R "$REPO"`, `gh pr view` has no repository context to resolve PR_NUM from and the manual review workflow fails before checkout.

findings.title: Keep the repository explicit for manual PR resolution
findings.whyItBreaks: For workflow_dispatch, EVENT_HEAD is empty and execution enters lines 38-39 before any checkout. The unscoped `gh pr view` commands must infer a repository from the runner directory; a clean runner has no repository context, while a persistent self-hosted worktree can point at the wrong one. The workflow then cannot obtain the requested PR SHAs.

findings.title: Restore explicit repository selection for manual PR lookup
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, EVENT_HEAD is empty, so this branch runs before any actions/checkout step. Without -R "$REPO" (or GH_REPO), gh pr view has no workflow-provided repository context to resolve PR_NUM from; it can fail to determine a repository or use stale self-hosted-runner checkout context. The requested manual review then never obtains head/base SHAs.

findings.title: Reject malformed review envelopes
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported parser now converts missing or wrongly typed required envelope fields into an empty summary and arrays, rather than surfacing malformed output. Callers therefore cannot distinguish an invalid reviewer response from a legitimate zero-finding review.

findings.title: Reject malformed review output instead of masking it
findings.whyItBreaks: Inputs such as null or an object missing findings/checked/residual_risks previously threw a malformed-output error, but now normalize to empty arrays and an empty summary. That result is indistinguishable from a legitimate review with no findings or blocking risks, so this exported API forces callers to treat failed or truncated review generation as a real review.

findings.title: Preserve errors for malformed top-level review output
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported normalizer now converts malformed top-level fields into empty defaults. For example, `{}` yields a zero-finding review rather than surfacing malformed output, making a truncated reviewer response indistinguishable from a valid empty review.

findings.title: Restore requested PR ref resolution for manual reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, github.event.pull_request is absent. Checkout falls back to github.ref (the workflow's selected branch), while PR_BASE_SHA and PR_HEAD_SHA are empty; the reviewer is therefore invoked for pr_number without that PR's checkout or diff refs.

findings.title: Resolve refs from the requested PR in manual runs
findings.whyItBreaks: `workflow_dispatch` events have no `pull_request` payload. The changed expressions therefore check out `github.ref`, fetch `main`, and pass empty `PR_BASE_SHA`/`PR_HEAD_SHA` values. The required `pr_number` input is used only as the CLI argument, so manually reviewing a PR with another head or base branch runs from incorrect refs.

findings.title: Restore PR ref resolution for manual dispatches
findings.whyItBreaks: For `workflow_dispatch`, `github.event.pull_request.*` is unset. The changed checkout therefore uses `github.ref` instead of the supplied `pr_number`'s head, fetches `main` instead of that PR's base branch, and passes empty `PR_BASE_SHA`/`PR_HEAD_SHA` to the review command at `.github/workflows/review.yml:47`. A manual run can consequently review the wrong revision.

findings.title: Preserve capacity for large Git diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: `runLocal` obtains the complete review patch through `git(["diff", baseSha, "HEAD"])` at line 100. Without `maxBuffer`, `spawnSync` uses its 1 MiB default; exceeding it produces a nonzero/null status that `git()` turns into a thrown error at lines 12-13. This regresses the prior 64 MiB limit and prevents local review of such diffs.

findings.title: Restore the diff buffer limit
findings.whyItBreaks: Without maxBuffer, spawnSync uses Node's 1 MiB default and terminates git when stdout exceeds that limit. runLocal obtains the complete review payload through `git diff` at src/adapters/local.ts:100, so a larger patch now throws rather than being reviewed.

findings.title: Restore the large buffer for git diff output
findings.whyItBreaks: Without maxBuffer, spawnSync defaults to a 1 MiB output buffer. runLocal reads the complete `git diff` into `patch`, so any review diff over that limit terminates with a buffer error and throws instead of submitting a review.

findings.title: Keep failed reviews as failed checks
findings.whyItBreaks: When review execution throws, this publishes a neutral check despite reporting that the review did not pass. A required Needlefish check can therefore be satisfied even though no review completed.

findings.title: Bind gh diff to the target repository
findings.whyItBreaks: PR metadata is fetched from the explicit GITHUB_REPOSITORY, but ghText does not receive cwd or a repository argument. If runGithub is called with a cwd different from the process repository, `gh pr diff <number>` can resolve the same PR number from that process repository while the result is posted to GITHUB_REPOSITORY.

findings.title: Report review errors as failures
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed catch path posts a completed Needlefish check with conclusion "neutral" when review() throws. GitHub accepts neutral as a successful required-check status, so a repository requiring this check can permit merging although the review failed.

findings.title: Keep review failures failing
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed catch path publishes a completed `Needlefish` check with conclusion `neutral` after `review()` or review posting throws. GitHub treats `neutral` as successful for required status checks, so repositories requiring this check can merge despite the review error.

findings.title: Keep PR code out of the privileged reviewer execution path
findings.whyItBreaks: The workflow checks out a same-repository PR head and executes its `src/cli.ts` with a token permitted to write pull requests and checks. A PR can modify that executed code to forge review results or use the token's write permissions.

findings.title: Restore a checkout containing the Needlefish package
findings.whyItBreaks: The checked-out HEAD tree has no `package.json`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, or `src/cli.ts`. The changed install command therefore cannot install a package, and the review job fails before reaching the reviewer.

findings.title: Keep the reviewer checkout trusted
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed command executes `src/cli.ts` from the PR-head checkout after installing that checkout's dependencies. This workflow grants `pull-requests: write` and `checks: write`, so a same-repository PR can alter the CLI or install inputs to forge/suppress review output or use the token to alter checks and review comments.

findings.title: Keep the reviewer code outside the PR checkout
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed workflow checks out the same-repository PR head and then runs `pnpm install` plus `src/cli.ts`. A PR can modify install scripts or the CLI, which will execute on the self-hosted runner with a token authorized for pull-request and check writes.

findings.title: Run the reviewer from a checkout that contains the tool
findings.whyItBreaks: HEAD contains only `.github/workflows/review.yml` and `.needlefish/answers.json`; it has no `package.json`, lockfile, or `src/cli.ts`. The changed install and CLI commands therefore cannot run successfully.

findings.title: Preserve case-insensitive severity normalization
findings.whyItBreaks: normalizeReview accepts untyped parsed output and maps each finding through normalizeFinding. A severity such as " p2 " or "p0" was previously normalized to P2/P0, but now fails the exact enum check and is emitted as P3, silently understating real findings.

findings.title: Restore severity canonicalization before validating
findings.whyItBreaks: normalizeReview passes untyped finding objects to this function. A semantically valid severity such as " p0 " or "p0" is now rejected and emitted as "P3", silently downgrading the finding.

findings.title: Preserve canonical severity normalization
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously `normalizeFinding({ severity: " p1 " })` returned `P1`; the changed exact-membership check now returns `P3`. This exported normalizer silently downgrades valid non-canonical severity inputs from existing callers.

findings.title: Keep GitHub tokens out of the Codex subprocess environment
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the explicit `env` option makes `spawnSync` inherit `process.env`, including GH_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN, and GITHUB_API_TOKEN that this wrapper previously removed. The repository-reviewing Codex subprocess can therefore access and potentially exfiltrate runner credentials.

findings.title: Keep GitHub credentials out of the Codex child process
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the explicit `env` option makes `spawnSync` inherit `process.env`, exposing `GH_TOKEN`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN` to the Codex subprocess. The base revision explicitly removed these credentials before crossing this boundary.

findings.title: Keep GitHub tokens out of the Codex subprocess
findings.whyItBreaks: Omitting `env` makes `spawnSync` inherit `process.env`, including `GH_TOKEN`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN`. This passes repository credentials into the Codex process while it analyzes repository-controlled content, restoring the secret-exposure path the removed code explicitly prevented.

findings.title: Keep forked PR code off the self-hosted runner
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the same-repository condition allows fork PRs into this self-hosted job. The job checks out the fork-controlled head SHA at .github/workflows/review.yml:20-24, installs its dependencies at :28, and executes its source at :34, allowing arbitrary code execution in the runner environment.

findings.title: Restore the same-repository guard for self-hosted execution
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the job-level guard allows fork PRs to run on `self-hosted`; the workflow checks out the fork-controlled head SHA, installs its dependencies, and executes its checked-out CLI code.

findings.title: Restore the same-repository guard for self-hosted execution
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the job-level condition allows fork PRs to reach this self-hosted job. The workflow checks out the fork head SHA, installs its dependencies, and executes its `src/cli.ts`; a fork author can therefore run arbitrary PR-controlled code on the self-hosted runner.

findings.title: Preserve zero as a valid runner duration
findings.whyItBreaks: A configured value of "0" previously produced a zero-delay timer. The new `parsed > 0` guard falls back to 5000 ms for `NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_GRACE_MS`, so a timed-out process configured for immediate SIGKILL waits an extra five seconds before escalation.

findings.title: Keep zero-valued runner timeout overrides valid
findings.whyItBreaks: Before this change, `0` was an accepted duration. Operators using `NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_GRACE_MS=0` to immediately escalate a timed-out process to SIGKILL now silently receive the 5000ms fallback instead; likewise, a zero SIGKILL give-up delay becomes 2000ms. This changes an existing environment configuration's timeout behavior without migration or replacement.

findings.title: Preserve zero as a valid runner-delay configuration
findings.whyItBreaks: A configured value of 0 previously scheduled the corresponding timeout immediately. It now silently becomes the fallback: NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_GRACE_MS=0 waits 5000 ms before SIGKILL, and NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_SIGKILL_GIVE_UP_MS=0 waits 2000 ms before resolving. This changes existing environment configuration semantics on upgrade.

findings.title: Review every changed file after capping hotspots
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed cap at src/core/review.ts:93 selects only six mapper hotspots after removing the tail-coverage backstop. Changed files absent from those hotspots receive no deep-review prompt at src/core/review.ts:97-105 and have no residual-risk record, allowing an incomplete large review to pass.

findings.title: Preserve blocking residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed assignment at src/core/review.ts:113 replaces residual risks returned by each deep review with an empty array before the critic is invoked at src/core/review.ts:115-118. A deep pass that reports blocks:true therefore cannot inform the final verdict derived at src/core/review.ts:122.

findings.title: Keep deep coverage for every changed file
findings.whyItBreaks: Large reviews execute deep passes only for the first six map hotspots. Removing the prior uncovered-file tail means a changed file omitted from those selected hotspots is never deep-reviewed, so defects in it cannot reach the final verdict.

findings.title: Preserve blocking residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: Deep-pass residual risks, including blocks=true risks, are replaced with an empty array before the critic runs. The critic and final verdict therefore cannot distinguish an incomplete or blocked deep review from a clean one, and can return a non-blocked result despite unresolved review coverage.

findings.title: Review every changed file after hotspot selection
findings.whyItBreaks: Large reviews cap mapped hotspots at six. Removing the tail-coverage hotspot means any changed file omitted by the mapper, or assigned to a seventh-or-later hotspot, is never deep-reviewed and cannot contribute findings or a blocking risk. The pipeline can therefore return an approving review while part of the submitted change was not reviewed.

findings.title: Preserve residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: A deep review can return residual_risks, including blocks=true, but this code replaces them with an empty array before passing the candidate to the critic. The critic and final deriveVerdict call no longer receive the unresolved-risk signal, so a large PR with incomplete evidence can be returned as non-blocking.

findings.title: Keep deep-pass failures as blocked review results
findings.whyItBreaks: A malformed or failed Codex response for any one hotspot now rejects reviewLarge immediately. Previously that failure was recorded as a blocking residual risk and the caller received a review result explaining that the affected files were not reviewed. Large-review requests now fail instead of yielding the safe blocked outcome.

findings.title: Preserve focus when constructing large-review prompts
findings.whyItBreaks: In the large-review path, the changed lines remove bundle.focus from the map-pass JSON and remove its {{FOCUS}} substitution from every deep-review prompt. Large bundles are selected by review() based on patch size or file count, so a caller-provided focus constraint reaches small-review prompts but is omitted from both large-review passes.

findings.title: Forward focus into every large-review deep prompt
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed deep-prompt construction no longer replaces {{FOCUS}}, so each large-review deep pass sends the unresolved placeholder to runCodex rather than bundle.focus. The same diff also omits focus from the map bundle, preventing both stages of the large-review path from honoring caller-provided focus.

findings.title: Normalize rounded seconds before formatting
findings.whyItBreaks: At 119500 ms, the changed calculation produces minutes=1 and seconds=60, rendering "1m 60s" rather than "2m 0s". At 59500 ms it renders "60s" rather than "1m 0s". These values are emitted in per-call statistics at src/shared/render.ts:75 and total duration output at src/shared/render.ts:80.

findings.title: Carry rounded seconds into minutes
findings.whyItBreaks: For 119500ms, this computes minutes=1 and seconds=60, rendering `1m 60s` instead of `2m 0s`. At 59500ms it similarly renders `60s` rather than the prior normalized `1m 0s`. Both per-call and total-duration output use this formatter.

findings.title: Carry rounded seconds into minutes
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed calculation derives minutes before rounding the remainder. At 59,500ms it returns `60s`, and at 119,500ms it returns `1m 60s`, instead of normalized durations (`1m 0s` and `2m 0s`). This output is rendered in per-call stats at src/shared/render.ts:75 and total duration stats at src/shared/render.ts:80.

findings.title: Reject zero in parsePositiveInteger
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed condition makes parsePositiveInteger("0", label) return 0 even though the exported function name and thrown error promise a positive integer. Callers can now receive zero where the API previously guaranteed a value greater than zero.

findings.title: Reject zero in parsePositiveInteger
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed guard permits `0`, although the exported function's name and error message promise a positive integer. Callers can receive zero where they rely on a strictly positive value.

findings.title: Reject zero to preserve the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed condition accepts `0`, while this exported function’s name and thrown error promise a positive integer. Callers can now receive zero where the API documents a positive value.
